The various schools of the old masters were represented by a Raphael Madonna, a Virgin by Leonardo da Vinci, a nymph by Correggio, a woman by Titian, an adoration of the Magi by Veronese, an assumption of the Virgin by Murillo, a Holbein portrait, a monk by Velazquez, a martyr by Ribera, a village fair by Rubens, two Flemish landscapes by Teniers, three little genre paintings by Gerard Dow, Metsu, and Paul Potter, two canvases by Gericault and Prud'hon, plus seascapes by Backhuysen and Vernet. Among the works of modern art were pictures signed by Delacroix, Ingres, Decamps, Troyon, Meissonier, Daubigny, etc., and some wonderful miniature statues in marble or bronze, modeled after antiquity's finest originals, stood on their pedestals in the corners of this magnificent museum. [RM-011-008]Some remarks on the orographical disposition of the moon must conclude the description of its continents, chains of mountains, isolated mountains, amphitheatres, and watercourses. The moon is like an immense Switzerland--a continual Norway, where Plutonic influence has done everything. This surface, so profoundly rugged, is the result of the successive contractions of the crust while the orb was being formed. The lunar disc is propitious for the study of great geological phenomena. According to the remarks of some astronomers, its surface, although more ancient than the surface of the earth, has remained newer. There there is no water to deteriorate the primitive relief, the continuous action of which produces a sort of general levelling. No air, the decomposing influence of which modifies orographical profiles. There Pluto's work, unaltered by Neptune's, is in all its native purity. It is the earth as she was before tides and currents covered her with layers of soil.

[SC-030-027]"Well, granting that, what became of him? The suppositions we might make are not numerous. They are confined to three. Either Harry Grant and his companions have found their way to the English colonies, or they have fallen into the hands of the natives, or they are lost in the immense wilds of Australia."
[SC-004-023]"They have refused?"
[ACH-014-031]"But he didn't do it," cried Hatteras violently; "and what he didn't do we will, with God's help!"
[SC-049-036]"Captain," said John, running to Will Halley, "we are on the breakers."

[TN-001-089]"A hundred fond kisses, my darling Hulda. Kiss Dame Hansen, and Joel, too, for me. In fancy, I imprint another kiss upon your brow, around which the shining crown of the brides of the Telemark will cast a saint-like halo. Once more, farewell, dearest Hulda, farewell!

The craft belongs to the "heavier than air" school of flight rather than to balloons. Robur trusts entirely to screws: "the simpler his contrivance the better. And the screws... had sufficed for all the needs of his flying machine. One series could hold it suspended in the air, the other could drive it along" (RC-007-022).
aliases: [Barbicane, President Barbicane]

Approaching the last open water, the travellers sight a great smoking peak, and the cry goes up: "It is a volcano!" (ACH-054-014). The Doctor, undismayed, reasons aloud, "Why, then, should not volcanoes be found near the North Pole?" (ACH-054-020), fitting the marvel into his science.

[FEM-020-090]Sometimes the platform pitched and tossed like a ship beaten by the waves. But the two heroes of the meeting were good sailors, and their vessel safely arrived in the port of [[tampa-town|Tampa Town]].

[RM-022-009]Still, notwithstanding the diligence with which the different machines were got ready, notwithstanding the considerable sums which were placed at the disposition of the Gun Club by the Government of the Union, five long days (five centuries) went by before the preparations were completed. During that time public opinion was excited to the highest point. Telegrams were incessantly exchanged all over the world through the electric wires and cables. The saving of [[barbicane|Barbicane]], Nicholl, and [[michel-ardan|Michel Ardan]] became an international business. All the nations that had subscribed to the enterprise of the Gun Club were equally interested in the safety of the travellers.

Built in the Turner yard, the aerostat was the club's central project, and its president Uncle Prudent staked the Institute's whole creed upon it. When he and Phil Evans vanished, the assembly even voted to suspend work on the Go-Ahead in their absence. (RC-021-015)

Tabor Island is a small, almost arid rock in the South Pacific, lying at 153° west longitude and 37° 11' south latitude. (MI-034-116) In *The Mysterious Island* it proves to be the nearest neighbor of [[Lincoln Island]], and its role in the plot links the colonists to the wider Vernean world through the marooned convict Ayrton.
region: North America (New York–Canada)
# Lake Barnett
description: 'New Zealand port on the 37th parallel toward which the Macquarie sails, keeping the searchers on Captain Grant''s latitude.'
- The land traversed in [[Michael Strogoff]]
- Its western gate, [[Gibraltar]]

The South Pole is the goal of the Nautilus's most audacious southern voyage in *[[Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea]]*. To reach it is framed as almost impossible, for it is "even more unattainable than the North Pole, which still hadn't been reached by the boldest navigators" (TTLU-037-054), yet Captain Nemo resolves to press on.

It is likewise the terminus of the guarded convoy. One official "had been appointed to accompany the convoy to Rio de Janeiro" (EHLA-039-018), and at the close "there was sent to Rio de Janeiro a detailed report of the whole affair" (EHLA-039-041), the city receiving both the prisoners and the truth of the crime.

For Verne's voyagers the isthmus is a matter of routes and reckonings. In [[The Waif of the Cynthia]] a captain weighs his passages — "I am not thinking of Panama, nor of Cape Horn, nor of the Cape of Good Hope" (WC-015-013) — setting the way of Panama beside the two great capes as a rival path between the oceans.
label: Pennsylvania

When Ayrton asked to live apart, it was agreed that a wooden house should be built at the corral, made as comfortable as possible. (MI-039-026) Ayrton took possession of the habitation that had been prepared for him. (MI-040-025)
When the pirates tried to wrest the phial from him, Roch hurled it to the ground and crushed the deflagrator under his foot, disarming his own terrible weapon (FF-017-083).
